policy_statement: Continue to incorporate City-owned green infrastructure into the Asset Management Plan.
mechanism_description: The city continues incorporating city-owned green infrastructure into its asset management plan to better prioritize upkeep and investment that improves stormwater and climate resilience.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (heavier rainfall/stormwater surges) → improved infrastructure performance/maintenance → ↓ flood/overflow exposure + ↓ vulnerability → ↓ climate impacts
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: The city formalizes green infrastructure as managed municipal assets within an asset management plan, improving inspection cycles, preventive maintenance, lifecycle renewal, and capital prioritization.  
(2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Well-maintained green infrastructure preserves/raises infiltration, detention, evapotranspiration, and filtration capacity, reducing peak runoff rates and volumes and lowering burden on gray drainage/sewer systems.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced stormwater flooding, combined sewer overflows, and runoff-related damages during extreme precipitation events—i.e., improved climate resilience (adaptation).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced exposure and damage from extreme precipitation via better-performing green stormwater infrastructure (improved stormwater resilience).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: managing and maintaining green infrastructure to handle increased stormwater loads reduces hazard impacts (flooding/overflow), which is adaptation.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while green infrastructure can have co-benefits (urban cooling, minor sequestration, energy savings), the explicit and largest intended effect in this mechanism is stormwater resilience under climate-driven precipitation extremes.  
3) Edge cases applied: if a policy framed the asset management primarily to maximize carbon sequestration or reduce operational emissions, mitigation/NBS could dominate; here the described outcome is stormwater/climate resilience, so adaptation governs.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: any policy that incorporates green infrastructure into asset management to prioritize upkeep/investment for stormwater resilience primarily functions by reducing climate hazard exposure/vulnerability.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., “flood risk management,” “nature-based adaptation,” “governance/planning”) is deferred to policy-level evidence and context.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could be co-classified conceptually with Nature-Based Solutions because it manages green infrastructure, but the dominant climate-relevant effect specified is resilience to stormwater impacts (adaptation). If the “green infrastructure” is mostly tree canopy/greenspace with primary goals of heat mitigation or carbon sink enhancement, categorization could shift.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as an internal governance/maintenance standard, but the explicit reference to incorporation into an "Asset Management Plan" most strongly signals a planning/asset-management strategy rather than a standalone regulatory or performance requirement.
